content.text: Azure compute services are the services that run application code. They span from full infrastructure control (virtual machines where you manage the OS, runtime, and everything above the hypervisor) to fully managed serverless functions (where Azure manages everything and you provide only the code). Choosing the right compute service determines your operational overhead, scaling behavior, and cost profile.

attrs.alt: Azure compute services on the control spectrum: Virtual Machines (IaaS) → VM Scale Sets → App Service (PaaS) → AKS → Azure Functions (serverless)

content.text: Azure Virtual Machines (VMs) are the foundational compute service. A VM is a software emulation of a physical computer: it has a virtual CPU, RAM, disk, and network adapter, all backed by Azure's physical infrastructure. You choose the VM size (SKU), the operating system image, the storage type, and the network configuration. Once deployed, you manage the OS, its patches, the runtime environment, and the application.

VMs are the right choice when: the workload requires a specific OS configuration, the application relies on OS-level features not available in PaaS, you are lifting and shifting an existing on-premises application, or licensing requirements mandate a specific host type (dedicated hosts for SQL Server, for example).
Dev/test, low baseline with spike tolerance

# Create a Linux VM
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --location eastus \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D2s_v5 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys \
  --public-ip-sku Standard

# Create a Windows Server VM
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWinVM \
  --image Win2022Datacenter \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --admin-username adminuser \
  --admin-password "SecureP@ssword1!"

# List available VM sizes in a region
az vm list-sizes --location eastus --output table | head -20

# Stop (deallocate) a VM to stop billing for compute
az vm deallocate --resource-group myRG --name myVM

# Start a deallocated VM
az vm start --resource-group myRG --name myVM

# Resize a VM (must be deallocated first)
az vm deallocate --resource-group myRG --name myVM
az vm resize --resource-group myRG --name myVM --size Standard_D4s_v5
az vm start --resource-group myRG --name myVM

VM Scale Sets manage a group of identical, load-balanced VMs that can automatically scale in and out based on demand or a schedule. VMSS is the compute foundation for Azure Kubernetes Service node pools, Azure Batch, and Service Fabric clusters. It is also used directly for stateless web tiers, game server fleets, and HPC job pools.

Scale Sets support two orchestration modes: Uniform (all VMs are identical, simpler scaling) and Flexible (VMs can have different configurations, required for Availability Zone placement). For most web and API workloads, Uniform mode is sufficient.

# Create a VMSS with autoscale
az vmss create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --instance-count 2 \
  --vm-sku Standard_D2s_v5 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys \
  --orchestration-mode Uniform \
  --zones 1 2 3  # Spread across Availability Zones

# Configure autoscale: scale out when CPU > 70%, scale in when CPU < 30%
az monitor autoscale create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --resource myVMSS \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets \
  --name autoscaleProfile \
  --min-count 2 \
  --max-count 20 \
  --count 2

az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --autoscale-name autoscaleProfile \
  --condition "Percentage CPU > 70 avg 5m" \
  --scale out 2

az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --autoscale-name autoscaleProfile \
  --condition "Percentage CPU < 30 avg 5m" \
  --scale in 1

# List all VMSS instances
az vmss list-instances \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --query "[].{name:name, state:provisioningState, zone:zones[0]}" \
  --output table

Azure App Service is a PaaS platform for hosting web applications, REST APIs, and WebSocket backends. You deploy code (or a container image), and Azure manages the underlying compute, OS patching, TLS certificate renewal, scaling, and deployment infrastructure. App Service supports .NET, Node.js, Python, Java, Ruby, and PHP runtimes natively, and any other language via custom Docker containers.

App Service plans determine the compute tier (Free, Shared, Basic, Standard, Premium, Isolated). The plan controls the VM size, auto-scaling, deployment slots, and whether the app runs on dedicated or shared infrastructure. Multiple apps can run on a single App Service plan, sharing the compute resources.

# Create an App Service plan (Standard tier, 2 instances)
az appservice plan create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAppPlan \
  --sku S2 \
  --is-linux

# Create a Python web app on the plan
az webapp create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --plan myAppPlan \
  --name mywebapp \
  --runtime "PYTHON:3.11"

# Deploy code from a local directory
az webapp up \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mywebapp \
  --runtime "PYTHON:3.11"

# Create a deployment slot for staging (blue/green deployments)
az webapp deployment slot create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mywebapp \
  --slot staging

# Swap staging to production (zero-downtime deployment)
az webapp deployment slot swap \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mywebapp \
  --slot staging \
  --target-slot production

# Scale out the App Service plan
az appservice plan update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAppPlan \
  --number-of-workers 4

Azure Kubernetes Service is a managed Kubernetes offering. Azure manages the Kubernetes control plane (the API server, scheduler, etcd cluster, and controller manager) at no charge. You manage the node pools (VM instances that run your containerized workloads). AKS integrates with Azure Active Directory for RBAC, Azure CNI or kubenet for networking, Azure Disk and Azure Files for persistent storage, and Azure Monitor for observability.

AKS is the right choice when you need container orchestration: pod scheduling, service discovery, rolling deployments, autoscaling (HPA, VPA, KEDA), and the ecosystem of Kubernetes tooling (Helm, Istio, Prometheus).

# Create an AKS cluster
az aks create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKSCluster \
  --location eastus \
  --node-count 3 \
  --node-vm-size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --zones 1 2 3 \
  --enable-managed-identity \
  --enable-addons monitoring \
  --network-plugin azure \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Get credentials to use kubectl
az aks get-credentials \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKSCluster

# Check cluster nodes
kubectl get nodes -o wide

# Add a GPU node pool for ML workloads
az aks nodepool add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myAKSCluster \
  --name gpupool \
  --node-count 2 \
  --node-vm-size Standard_NC6s_v3 \
  --node-taints "sku=gpu:NoSchedule"

# Enable cluster autoscaler on the default node pool
az aks nodepool update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myAKSCluster \
  --name nodepool1 \
  --enable-cluster-autoscaler \
  --min-count 2 \
  --max-count 20

Azure Functions is a serverless compute service. You write individual functions — small, focused pieces of code triggered by events (HTTP requests, timer, Service Bus messages, Blob Storage uploads, Event Hub events). Azure manages all infrastructure: there are no VMs to configure, no OS to patch, and no need to think about capacity. With the Consumption plan, billing is per execution and per GB-second of memory consumed — there is no charge when no functions are running.

Azure Functions supports C#, JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, Java, PowerShell, and Go (via custom handlers). Each function has one trigger and optional input/output bindings that connect it to storage, messaging, and other services without writing integration code.

# Create a Function App (Consumption plan — serverless, billing per execution)
az functionapp create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --consumption-plan-location eastus \
  --runtime python \
  --runtime-version 3.11 \
  --functions-version 4 \
  --name myFunctionApp \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount \
  --os-type linux

# Create a Function App on Premium plan (always-warm, no cold starts)
az appservice plan create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myPremiumPlan \
  --sku EP1 \
  --is-linux

az functionapp create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --plan myPremiumPlan \
  --runtime python \
  --runtime-version 3.11 \
  --functions-version 4 \
  --name myPremiumFunctionApp \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount

# Deploy a function app from local project (requires Azure Functions Core Tools)
# func azure functionapp publish myFunctionApp

# View function app settings
az functionapp config appsettings list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myFunctionApp \
  --output table

# View invocation logs
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --offset 1h \
  --query "[?resourceType.value=='Microsoft.Web/sites'].{time:eventTimestamp, op:operationName.localizedValue}" \
  --output table
Beyond the five primary services above, Azure provides specialized compute for specific scenarios:

run containers without managing VMs or a Kubernetes cluster. ACI is billed per container-second with sub-second startup. Appropriate for burst containers, CI/CD runners, short-lived processing jobs, or Kubernetes virtual node overflow.

managed service for large-scale parallel and HPC jobs. Batch provisions, scales, and manages VM pools for you; you submit jobs with task lists. Used for rendering, financial risk simulation, scientific computing, and media transcoding.
fully managed PaaS for Java Spring applications. Manages the Spring Cloud infrastructure (Eureka, Config Server, Zipkin) in addition to hosting application code.
an App Service deployment that runs inside a dedicated VNet for network isolation and compliance. Supports very high density of apps on dedicated compute.

single-tenant physical servers in Azure. The VM SKUs running on a Dedicated Host are isolated to that physical server — no other customer's VMs share the host. Required for some regulatory (HIPAA, GDPR) or licensing (Windows Server BYOL with Software Assurance) scenarios.
Run existing application without code changes (lift and shift)
Stateless web or API application in any common language
Large fleet of identical stateless VMs with autoscale
One-off container execution without cluster overhead
Regulated workload requiring dedicated physical isolation
Azure compute services offer several pricing models that balance cost against flexibility and commitment. Understanding these models is critical for architecting cost-effective solutions.
Billed per second for compute used; no commitment
Commit to a VM size in a region for 1 or 3 years
Commit to a hourly spend for 1 or 3 years; applies across services
Flexible workloads that may change VM size or region
Unused Azure capacity at up to 90% discount; can be evicted with 30s notice
Fault-tolerant batch jobs, CI/CD workers, stateless burst
Use existing Windows Server or SQL Server licenses with Software Assurance
Organizations with existing Microsoft EA licensing
Reduced rates for non-production environments under MSDN subscriptions

# Check the effective price of a VM size in a region (requires billing reader role)
az vm list-skus \
  --location eastus \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --query "[].{name:name, tier:tier, capabilities:capabilities[?name=='vCPUs'].value | [0]}" \
  --output table

# Create a Spot VM (with eviction policy set to Deallocate, so the OS disk is preserved)
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySpotVM \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --priority Spot \
  --eviction-policy Deallocate \
  --max-price 0.10 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Create a VMSS with Spot instances and mixed on-demand fallback
az vmss create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myMixedVMSS \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --vm-sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --priority Spot \
  --eviction-policy Deallocate \
  --instance-count 5 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Enable Azure Hybrid Benefit on a Windows VM (saves Windows Server license cost)
az vm update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWinVM \
  --license-type Windows_Server

# Check if Hybrid Benefit is applied
az vm show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWinVM \
  --query "licenseType" \
  --output tsv

# List all VMs in a resource group with their license type
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{name:name, size:hardwareProfile.vmSize, licenseType:licenseType, powerState:powerState}" \
  --output table

Azure compute services provide multiple layers of fault tolerance, from hardware fault domains within a datacenter to zone-redundant deployments across independent facilities. Understanding the SLA tiers is essential for architecting reliable systems.

# Create an Availability Set (classic HA for VMs in a single datacenter)
az vm availability-set create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAvSet \
  --platform-fault-domain-count 3 \
  --platform-update-domain-count 5

# Create a VM in the Availability Set
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name web-01 \
  --availability-set myAvSet \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Check the fault domain and update domain assigned to a VM
az vm get-instance-view \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name web-01 \
  --query "{faultDomain:instanceView.platformFaultDomain, updateDomain:instanceView.platformUpdateDomain}" \
  --output json

# Create VMs across Availability Zones (zone 1 and zone 2)
for ZONE in 1 2 3; do
  az vm create \
    --resource-group myRG \
    --name "web-0${ZONE}" \
    --zone "$ZONE" \
    --image Ubuntu2204 \
    --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
    --admin-username azureuser \
    --generate-ssh-keys \
    --no-wait
done

# Wait for all three to finish
az vm wait --resource-group myRG --name web-01 --created
az vm wait --resource-group myRG --name web-02 --created
az vm wait --resource-group myRG --name web-03 --created

# Verify zones assigned to each VM
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{name:name, zone:zones[0]}" \
  --output table

# Create a zone-redundant App Service plan (Premium v3 or Isolated v2 required)
az appservice plan create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myZonePlan \
  --sku P1v3 \
  --is-linux \
  --zone-redundant
Every Azure compute service connects to Azure virtual networking, but the integration depth and configuration options vary by service tier and compute type.
NICs on each instance in subnet
Regional VNet Integration (one-way: outbound to VNet)
Dedicated subnet in customer VNet (fully injected)
Azure CNI (pods get subnet IPs) or kubenet (pod NAT)
Via node NIC or NAT Gateway
Regional VNet Integration for outbound only

# Add VNet Integration to an App Service (allows app to reach private resources in VNet)
az webapp vnet-integration add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mywebapp \
  --vnet myVNet \
  --subnet AppServiceSubnet

# Verify VNet integration is active
az webapp vnet-integration list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mywebapp \
  --output table

# Add a Private Endpoint to an App Service (makes the app reachable only from VNet)
az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAppPrivateEndpoint \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet PrivateEndpointSubnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id $(az webapp show -g myRG -n mywebapp --query id -o tsv) \
  --group-id sites \
  --connection-name mywebapp-connection

# Create a private DNS zone for App Service private endpoints
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "privatelink.azurewebsites.net"

az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --zone-name "privatelink.azurewebsites.net" \
  --name myDNSLink \
  --virtual-network myVNet \
  --registration-enabled false

# Create AKS cluster with Azure CNI (pods get VNet IPs directly)
az aks create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKSCluster \
  --network-plugin azure \
  --vnet-subnet-id $(az network vnet subnet show -g myRG --vnet-name myVNet -n AKSSubnet --query id -o tsv) \
  --service-cidr 10.100.0.0/16 \
  --dns-service-ip 10.100.0.10 \
  --node-count 3 \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Enable private cluster API server (makes kubectl talk via private link only)
az aks create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myPrivateAKS \
  --enable-private-cluster \
  --private-dns-zone system \
  --node-count 3 \
  --generate-ssh-keys

All Azure compute services emit metrics and logs to Azure Monitor. The key difference is where telemetry originates: infrastructure metrics (CPU, disk, network) are collected by the Azure platform automatically; application-level metrics require the Azure Monitor agent (for VMs) or SDK instrumentation (for Functions and App Service).

# Enable the Azure Monitor Agent on a VM (replaces the legacy Log Analytics agent)
az vm extension set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vm-name myVM \
  --name AzureMonitorLinuxAgent \
  --publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor \
  --version 1.0 \
  --settings '{"workspaceId":"<LAW_ID>"}'

# Create a Data Collection Rule to send VM metrics to Log Analytics
az monitor data-collection rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name vmDataCollectionRule \
  --location eastus \
  --data-flows '[{"streams":["Microsoft-Perf","Microsoft-Syslog"],"destinations":["myLAW"]}]' \
  --destinations '{"logAnalytics":[{"workspaceResourceId":"<LAW_ID>","name":"myLAW"}]}'

# Associate the DCR with the VM
az monitor data-collection rule association create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name vmDCRAssociation \
  --rule-id $(az monitor data-collection rule show -g myRG -n vmDataCollectionRule --query id -o tsv) \
  --resource $(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv)

# Query VM CPU metrics over the last hour (Azure Monitor Metrics)
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource $(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv) \
  --metric "Percentage CPU" \
  --interval PT5M \
  --start-time "$(date -u -d '-1 hour' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" \
  --end-time "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" \
  --query "value[0].timeseries[0].data[-12:].{time:timeStamp, cpu:average}" \
  --output table

# Enable Application Insights on an App Service
az webapp config appsettings set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mywebapp \
  --settings APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING="InstrumentationKey=<key>;IngestionEndpoint=https://eastus.in.applicationinsights.azure.com/"

# View App Service HTTP logs via Log Analytics (after diagnostic settings enabled)
# az monitor log-analytics query \
#   --workspace <LAW_ID> \
#   --analytics-query "AppServiceHTTPLogs | where TimeGenerated > ago(1h) | summarize count() by ScStatus | order by count_ desc" \
#   --output table

# Get AKS cluster diagnostic logs (API server, scheduler, controller-manager)
az aks enable-addons \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKSCluster \
  --addons monitoring \
  --workspace-resource-id $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myLAW --query id -o tsv)

# View Azure Function invocation failures in the last hour
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Web/sites \
  --status Failed \
  --offset 1h \
  --query "[].{time:eventTimestamp, op:operationName.value, status:status.value}" \
  --output table

Azure's compute services span from full IaaS control (Virtual Machines) to fully managed serverless (Azure Functions). The choice depends on how much operational control you need, your workload's runtime requirements, and your team's operational capabilities.
Virtual Machines: full OS control, lift-and-shift workloads, specific OS or licensing requirements.
VM Scale Sets: horizontally scaled fleets of identical VMs with autoscale, used for web tiers and HPC.
App Service: managed PaaS for web apps and APIs; no OS management required; supports deployment slots for zero-downtime releases.
AKS: managed Kubernetes for containerized workloads; Azure manages the control plane; you manage node pools and workloads.
Azure Functions: serverless, event-driven code; Consumption plan scales to zero; billed per execution.
For specialized needs: ACI (quick container runs), Azure Batch (HPC), Spring Apps (Java), Dedicated Hosts (physical isolation).
